notes: Prose is largely verbatim from the entity-matched Wikipedia page (May 1969, Impulse!, AS 9181, Pitchfork #53 all confirmed). MB search match (sim 1.0) confirms 1969, impulse! AS-9181; keyTracks match ('The Creator Has a Master Plan' matches Parts I/II; 'Colors' exact). 'The most commercially successful spiritual jazz album' is an editorial superlative not strictly checkable, but wiki supports exceptional popularity ('mainstream FM radio airplay, surely the closest the avant-garde movement came to a "hit"').

MB embedded_mbid (authoritative for artist credit) co-credits the release to Hampton Hawes AND Martial Solal; ours bills Hawes alone and the prose ('a compelling contemporary jazz statement by Hampton Hawes') never mentions Solal. Treated as primary-artist billing / incomplete credit rather than a contradiction (Hawes is genuinely a credited artist), so not factual_error - but a repair-writer should surface the co-credit on this two-pianist album. Everything else verifies: BYG Records, 1969, all 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist. No Wikipedia page found; wikipediaUrl is null.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: It was Monk's first album for Columbia following his five-year recording period with Riverside Records.

wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: shortDescription: "1963 studio album by Thelonious Monk" (page: Monk's Dream (Thelonious Monk album))

Only the albumDNA prose and the stored wikipediaUrl belong to Monk's Dream (1963). Repair: rewrite prose for Monk's Blues (1969 big-band Columbia/CBS album) and replace or drop the wiki link.

MB (sim 1.0) confirms title/artist/1970/Columbia and all 4 keyTracks (incl. 'Pharaoh's Dance'); no Wikipedia evidence (opensearch empty, no stored URL). Right entity beyond doubt, no contradictions, but many substantive claims are uncheckable against the pack: double-album format, August 1969 recording, personnel (Shorter, Corea, Zawinul, McLaughlin, Holland, multiple drummers), Teo Macero's editing, Mati Klarwein cover, RIAA Gold 1976 / Platinum 2003, ~400,000 first-year sales, Santana/Radiohead citations. wrongClaims.ourClaim: Chris McGregor's Brotherhood of Breath is the debut album by South African pianist and composer Chris McGregor's big band of the same name.
wrongClaims.problem: Prose describes an album titled 'Chris McGregor's Brotherhood of Breath' (1971, Neon/RCA), not an album titled 'Jazz in Africa'. No evidence that an album called 'Jazz in Africa' by this band exists: MB search returned zero candidates.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: resolvedTitle: "Chris McGregor's Brotherhood of Breath"; shortDescription: "1971 studio album by Chris McGregor's Brotherhood of Breath"

notes: Identity tangle. Ours.title 'Jazz in Africa' has NO supporting evidence anywhere (MB searchCandidates empty; the stored wiki page is about the differently-titled debut album). The prose, the Joe Boyd / Neon-RCA / Fledg'ling details, and 3 of 4 keyTracks (Davashe's Dream, MRA, Andromeda) all belong to the 1971 debut 'Chris McGregor's Brotherhood of Breath'; keyTrack 'Kongi's Theme' is on neither tracklist in evidence. Two repair paths: (a) retitle the item to the debut album (then fix year to 1971, label to Neon/RCA, drop Kongi's Theme), or (b) the intended entity really is some 'Jazz in Africa' release - background memory (NOT citable) suggests 'Jazz in Africa' is a Jazz Epistles title and 'Kongi's Theme' comes from McGregor's 1963 'Jazz: The African Sound'; a human should check. wikiLinkVerdict 'unsure' because the page is plausibly the intended album but does not match the shipped title.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: The Art Ensemble of Chicago is an avant-garde jazz group that grew out of the Association for the Advancement of Creative Musicians (AACM) in the late 1960s.
wrongClaims.problem: The bulk of the prose describes the BAND (AACM origins, little instruments, costumes and face paint, 500 instruments in Europe 1969), not the album Les Stances a Sophie. Content about a group when the item is an album; stored wikipediaUrl is the band article.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: shortDescription: "American avant-garde jazz group" (page: Art Ensemble of Chicago)

notes: The item itself resolves fine: MB (sim 1.0) matches the 1970 soundtrack release and ALL 6 keyTracks. The band page even confirms the album exists ('Les Stances a Sophie with singer Fontella Bass... the soundtrack to the French movie of the same title', 1970). The final two prose sentences ARE album-specific and consistent ('Theme de Yoyo' is the opening track), but the factual skeleton is the band bio. Label caveat: ours says Pathe; the MB evidence release is a US Nessa Records pressing (1970) - the original French label is not in evidence, so Pathe is unconfirmed but NOT contradicted (labels are pressing-specific); do not auto-change. Repair: rewrite prose about the album; link the album's wiki page if one exists.
